questionText: Solve $\sec x = 4$ for $0^\circ \le x < 360^\circ$, giving your answers to one decimal place.

steps.explanation: Secant is the reciprocal of cosine, so we convert the equation into one for cosine.
Taking reciprocals of both sides gives $\cos x = 0.25$; this is not a standard exact angle, so we will use the inverse cosine.
The value $0.25$ lies within $[-1,1]$, so real solutions exist.

steps.description: Find the principal value with arccos
steps.explanation: The inverse cosine returns the principal value, an angle in $[0^\circ,180^\circ]$. Here $\arccos(0.25) \approx 75.5^\circ$ to one decimal place.
Since $75.5^\circ$ is acute it lies in the first quadrant, giving our first solution and also serving as the reference angle.

steps.workingLatex: \cos x > 0 \text{ in quadrants I and IV}
steps.explanation: The cosine value is positive, and cosine is positive in the first and fourth quadrants, so there is a second solution in quadrant IV.
The first-quadrant solution is the principal value itself.
In the fourth quadrant we subtract the reference angle from $360^\circ$, giving about $284.5^\circ$.

steps.description: Confirm both lie in the interval
steps.explanation: Both angles satisfy $0^\circ \le x < 360^\circ$, so both are kept.

steps.description: Check a solution in the original equation
steps.explanation: Substituting back gives approximately $4$, confirming the answer; $284.5^\circ$ checks the same way since it has the same cosine.
These two angles, to one decimal place, are the complete set of solutions in the interval.
